title: How to Use NanoClaw (Better Than OpenClaw?)
summary: NanoClaw is a lightweight, AI-native alternative to OpenClaw designed for orchestrating agent swarms through Telegram with ephemeral Docker containers. It supports frameworks like Claude Code for customization and is built to be forked and rewritten by AI rather than requiring manual modification of large codebases.
writeup: NanoClaw is a lightweight, AI-native alternative to OpenClaw that runs every agent action inside an ephemeral Docker container, destroyed after use, making it safer to run on a primary machine than OpenClaw's device-wide approach. Setup is driven entirely by Claude Code via a `/setup` command, and the project's own README instructs users to fork the repo and have Claude Code rewrite it to their needs. The video walks through installation, Telegram integration, and the beginnings of an agent swarm pipeline intended to scrape X via Apify, research the web, and produce a solution document. The core argument is that an AI-native, bespoke-by-design codebase is more tractable than a 430,000-line batteries-included monolith.

key_points: NanoClaw's architecture is built around ephemeral Docker containers: each incoming message spawns a container, the LLM processes the prompt inside it, the response streams out, and the container is then destroyed, with state surviving only through CLAUDE.md and explicitly mounted host directories.

OpenClaw's 430,000-line codebase runs directly on the host device with broad file-system access, while NanoClaw is designed to be sandboxed and safe to run on a primary computer, not a dedicated Raspberry Pi or Mac Mini.

NanoClaw explicitly targets power users who want to own and understand their full stack, in contrast to OpenClaw's batteries-included approach for users who just want a working assistant without understanding the internals.

Setup requires Docker, a Claude Code subscription, and three commands: git clone, cd nanoclaw, then invoking Claude and running the /setup slash command, which handles channel configuration and directory permissions interactively.

NanoClaw claims to be the first AI assistant to support agent swarms, and the Telegram swarm feature provisions multiple bots as a coordinated agent pool, with Claude Code performing the coding work during setup automatically.

The intended production pipeline demonstrated is: scrape X (Twitter) for trending AI pain points via Apify, research the web for alternate perspectives and solutions, then produce a written document, all coordinated through the agent swarm.
